did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used computer vision and pattern recognition algorithms, specifically the ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF) feature-matching method and co-registration-based algorithms, to detect changes in dermoscopic images of naevi over time for melanoma screening support.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a novel decision support system using pattern detection (ORB and co-registration algorithms) to support dermoscopic screening. It describes specific methods involving image augmentation of PH2 and HAM10000 datasets, reports quantitative results (specificity, sensitivity, margins), and proposes a new workflow for supporting staff, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Decision support system based on pattern detection in iterative dermoscopic screening
journal: Computer Methods and Programs in Biomedicine
brief_summary: This study developed a decision support system for iterative dermoscopic screening to assist specialists in detecting melanoma by identifying changes in naevi over time rather than replacing the dermatologist. The researchers utilized pattern detection methods, specifically the ORB feature-matching algorithm and co-registration-based algorithms, to compare dermoscopic images. Since no longitudinal dataset existed, the authors augmented images from the PH2 and HAM10000 datasets to simulate the passage of time between visits. The evaluation focused on specificity and sensitivity, setting a true positive rate of 99% for both methods. The ORB method achieved a specificity of 97% on the PH2 dataset and 89% on the HAM10000 dataset, whereas the co-registration algorithm yielded significantly lower specificities of 23% and 19%, respectively. The authors conclude that the ORB-based approach allows supporting staff to perform initial screenings effectively, thereby reducing examination time and costs while maintaining high accuracy for detecting suspicious changes.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on detecting changes in naevi to support melanoma screening. The PH2 dataset included 40 melanomas. The algorithm was tuned to achieve a 99% true positive rate for malignant tumors/suspicious lesions.

The study analyzes changes in naevi over time. The PH2 dataset included 80 common naevi and 80 atypical naevi. The HAM10000 dataset contained images of naevi. The goal was to reject benign naevi without changes (97% specificity on PH2, 89% on HAM10000).

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of whether a naevus has changed (suspicious) or remained stable (non-suspicious) between iterative dermoscopic visits.
all_task_types.details: The study performs binary classification to detect changes in naevi over time. The model predicts a discrete label: 'suspicious' (indicating a new or changed naevus requiring specialist review) or 'non-suspicious' (indicating no change). The classification is based on pattern detection metrics (ORB feature matching or co-registration correlation) compared against a fixed margin/threshold. The primary evaluation metric is specificity at a fixed true positive rate (sensitivity) of 99%. The task is not regression, as the output is a binary decision for screening triage, not a continuous risk score or measurement.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Database
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image database obtained from the Dermatology Service of Hospital Pedro Hispano (Matosinhos, Portugal) as part of the 'Automatic computer-based Diagnosis system for Dermoscopy Images' project. Images were taken using the Tuebinger Mole Analyzer system (20x magnification) and stored as 8-bit RGB bitmaps (765 × 573 pixels) with lesion masks (ROI). The study used images from this dataset, augmenting them by rotation to simulate longitudinal changes.

use.testing: Used for testing the decision support system algorithms (ORB and co-registration) after image augmentation (rotation) to simulate time flow between visits.
use.other: Images were augmented by rotating them at 24 different angles to simulate the passage of time and varying acquisition conditions, as no longitudinal dataset existed.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: The 'Human Against Machine with 10000 training images' dataset, containing dermoscopic images of naevi gathered by the Department of Dermatology at the Medical University of Vienna, Austria, and the skin cancer practice of Cliff Rosendahl in Queensland, Australia. Images were collected over 20 years using various devices and techniques, resulting in diverse resolutions, qualities, and skin types. The study used images from this dataset, augmenting them by rotation to simulate longitudinal changes.

use.testing: Used for testing the decision support system algorithms (ORB and co-registration) after image augmentation (rotation) to simulate time flow between visits.
use.other: Images were augmented by rotating them at 24 different angles to simulate the passage of time and varying acquisition conditions, as no longitudinal dataset existed.

statistical_methods_used.method: Fixed threshold setting based on True Positive Rate
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine the classification margin (threshold) for the ORB and co-registration algorithms, ensuring a true positive rate of 99% for detecting malignant tumors or changed naevi.
To evaluate the performance of the decision support system by measuring the proportion of benign/stable naevi correctly rejected (not flagged as suspicious) at the fixed 99% TPR.
To visualize the distribution of matching performance values for correct matches (rotated images) versus incorrect matches (different images) and assess the separation between the two classes.
To summarize the classification results (true positives, false positives, etc.) for the ORB and co-registration methods on the PH2 and HAM10000 datasets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance was evaluated using specificity and sensitivity at a fixed true positive rate (99%) threshold.

PH2.notes: The true positive rate was fixed at 99% for both methods. The ORB method showed clear separation in histogram analysis, while the co-registration method did not show ideal separation. The margin for ORB was 40, and for co-registration was 83.

HAM10000.notes: The true positive rate was fixed at 99% for both methods. The ORB method rejected 89% of naevi without changes. The co-registration method rejected 19% of naevi without changes. The margin for ORB was 40, and for co-registration was 80.

architecture_used.main_model: Pattern detection algorithms: ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF) feature-matching and co-registration-based algorithms.

orb_method.description: Feature matching using characteristic points (key points) search.

training_details.details: No neural network training or parameter learning was performed. The system relies on fixed algorithmic parameters. Thresholds (margins) were determined post-hoc to achieve a fixed True Positive Rate (TPR) of 99%.

classification_or_output_head.details: Classification is based on comparing matching performance values against a fixed margin/threshold. If the value is below the margin, the naevus is marked as suspicious (changed/new); if above, it is marked as non-suspicious (stable).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF) feature-matching algorithm
U-Net architecture (mentioned in literature review as a baseline/comparison context)
Fully Convolutional Neural Networks (FCN) (mentioned in literature review as a baseline/comparison context)
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) (mentioned in literature review as a baseline/comparison context)
preprocessing_techniques: Conversion to greyscale using weighted average method (Red*0.299 + Green*0.587 + Blue*0.114)
Hair removal using 'black top-hat' morphology method with a 17x17 cross-shaped kernel
Inpainting of removed hair regions using the Navier–Stokes algorithm with radius set to 1
Removal of lens shadows (black areas in image corners)
Histogram equalization to utilize all available intensity values for the naevus and surrounding skin
Region of Interest (ROI) extraction by finding contours using the Laplacian method, followed by morphological dilation, merging overlapping edges, and removing small surrounding objects

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images from the PH2 and HAM10000 datasets were rotated at 24 different angles to simulate the passage of time and varying acquisition conditions (device rotation and positioning) for iterative dermoscopic screening, as no longitudinal dataset existed.

conclusions: The proposed decision support system, which uses pattern detection (specifically the ORB feature-matching algorithm) to identify changes in naevi over time, allows supporting staff to perform initial screenings without expert help, thereby decreasing examination time and costs while increasing accessibility.

The ORB-based method demonstrated superior performance compared to the co-registration-based algorithm, achieving a specificity of 97% on the PH2 dataset and 89% on the HAM10000 dataset at a fixed true positive rate (sensitivity) of 99%.

The co-registration algorithm showed significantly lower performance, with specificities of only 23% (PH2) and 19% (HAM10000) at the same 99% true positive rate, making it less suitable for this application.

The ORB method provides a clear separation between matched and unmatched images in histogram analysis, whereas the co-registration method lacks ideal separation, indicating that feature-matching is a more suitable solution for detecting suspicious changes.

The study acknowledges a limitation regarding the lack of longitudinal patient data; consequently, the authors conclude that complete validation and final conclusions require future work involving accurate data collected from patients observed over long periods under realistic conditions.

The approach demonstrates that diagnostic support can be effectively achieved using feature-matching algorithms instead of deep learning models, avoiding the need for extensive training databases and mitigating issues with overfitting and generalization to new data.
important_remarks: The study lacks longitudinal patient data; changes in naevi were simulated by rotating static images from the PH2 and HAM10000 datasets, which limits the clinical validity of the results regarding actual temporal changes.

The co-registration algorithm demonstrated significantly poor performance (specificity of 23% on PH2 and 19% on HAM10000) compared to the ORB method, indicating it is unsuitable for this specific application.
The ORB method achieved a specificity of 97% on the PH2 dataset and 89% on the HAM10000 dataset at a fixed true positive rate (sensitivity) of 99%, showing good separation in histogram analysis.
The evaluation relies on image rotation to simulate time flow, which may not adequately represent real-world variations in lighting, angle, or minor lesion changes during follow-up visits.
The study acknowledges that complete validation and final conclusions require future work involving accurate data collected from patients observed over long periods under realistic conditions.

The approach avoids the need for extensive training databases and mitigates issues with overfitting common in deep learning models, but its generalization to new data types remains to be validated in a clinical setting.
